nativeRange.habitat: Wet shorelines, ditches, swamps, and shallow water margins; bog-edge plant in nature, sometimes briefly submersed in shallow water.
nativeRange.biotope: Eastern North American wetland margins and ditches

summary: A lesser-known North American bog-edge stem plant with fresh light-green, serrated leaves. Generally considered easy to grow, though reportedly more reliable emersed than fully submerged long-term.
careNotes: Penthorum sedoides is a North American native of the small family Penthoraceae (only two species worldwide). In nature it grows as a 30-60 cm tall perennial along wet shorelines, swamps, and ditches in eastern USA and Canada, and is winter-hardy. Submersed forms in the aquarium tend to be smaller (around 10-30 cm), more compact, and more densely leafed than emersed growth, with fresh light-green, slightly serrated, lanceolate leaves and abundant side shoots. With higher light it has been reported to take on a creeping habit suitable for the midground; under lower light it grows more upright with greater internodal spacing. Generally considered easy to maintain, but it is lesser-known in the aquarium trade and is reportedly more reliable when grown emersed (e.g., paludarium, ripariums, wabi-kusa, or pond margins) than as a fully submerged long-term aquarium plant. Cross-source spread on parameters: temperature 4-30°C across sources (Flowgrow's low end reflects winter pond hardiness rather than aquarium use; aquarium-relevant range used here), pH 5-8, hardness very tolerant (0-14 dKH). CO2 is not strictly required but supplemental CO2 (20-40 mg/L per Flowgrow) and nutrient-rich substrate appear to support denser, more compact submersed growth. Cold-tolerant and US native, so it is also suitable for outdoor temperate ponds and unheated coldwater tanks.
